title: Blumea viscosa
intro: Blumea viscosa, also known as sticky thistle or sticky chrysanthemum, is a perennial plant native to tropical Asia, belonging to the family Asteraceae. The plant has been traditionally used in Ayurvedic and Unani medicine for its health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antipyretic properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Blumea viscosa include flavonoids, terpenoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is commonly prepared as a decoction, powder, or infusion, and is often used in herbal remedies such as cough syrups, expectorants, and anti-inflammatory ointments.
taxonomy: Blumea viscosa is classified under the family Asteraceae, also known as the daisy or sunflower family. This family is a large and diverse group of flowering plants. Within the family Asteraceae, Blumea viscosa is further classified under the tribe Inuleae. The genus Blumea is a group of plants that are native to tropical and subtropical regions. The classification of Blumea viscosa reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the same family and genus.
uses: Blumea viscosa has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties. It has been employed to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. The plant's leaves are also used in traditional medicine to reduce inflammation and alleviate pain. In Ayurvedic medicine, Blumea viscosa is believed to have antiseptic and antibacterial properties, which help in treating wounds and skin infections. Further research is needed to confirm the plant's efficacy and safety for medicinal use.

Terpenoids, like sesquiterpenes, have been found to have antimicrobial and antifungal properties. The saponins present in the plant are known for their expectorant and anti-inflammatory effects.
